q: A push or a pull on an object is called:
sol: Force is defined as a push or pull that can change the state of motion or shape of an object.

q: Which of the following is a non-contact force?
sol: Magnetic force acts even when objects are not touching, making it a non-contact force.

q: The SI unit of force is:
sol: The Newton (N) is the standard unit named after Sir Isaac Newton.

q: If two equal forces act on an object in opposite directions, the net force is:
sol: Opposite forces of equal magnitude cancel each other out.

q: Pressure is defined as:
sol: Pressure is the force acting perpendicularly on a unit area of a surface.

q: Which force is responsible for objects falling to the ground?
sol: Gravity is the attractive force exerted by the Earth on all objects.

q: A camel can walk easily in the desert because:
sol: Broad feet increase the area, which decreases the pressure exerted on the sand.

q: The force that opposes the motion of one surface over another is:
sol: Friction always acts in the direction opposite to the motion.

q: When a plastic comb is rubbed with dry hair, it acquires:
sol: Rubbing creates a static electric charge, which exerts an electrostatic force.

q: Atmospheric pressure is measured using a:
sol: A barometer is the standard instrument for measuring the pressure of the air.

q: To increase pressure, we should:
sol: Pressure is inversely proportional to area; smaller area means higher pressure.

q: Muscular force is an example of:
sol: Muscular force can only be applied when in physical contact with an object.

q: The pressure exerted by a liquid ________ with depth.
sol: As depth increases, the weight of the liquid column above increases, thus increasing pressure.

q: Which of the following does not change the shape of an object?
o: A ball rolling on the floor
sol: Rolling a ball changes its position/motion, not its physical shape.

q: Force can change the ________ of a moving object.
sol: Force can accelerate, decelerate, or change the path of an object.

q: The unit of pressure is:
sol: 1 Pascal (Pa) is equal to 1 Newton per square metre (N/m²).

q: Magnetic force is a ________ force.
sol: Magnets attract or repel from a distance.

q: Why are the cutting edges of tools made sharp?
sol: Sharp edges have a very small area, making it easy to cut with less force.

q: An object will move only if the applied force is ________ than the frictional force.
sol: To overcome inertia and friction, the net force must be positive in the direction of motion.

q: The pressure at the bottom of a container depends on:
sol: Liquid pressure depends on depth and density, not the shape of the vessel.

q: Force of friction always acts ________ to the direction of motion.
sol: Friction works against the movement.

q: Atmospheric pressure is caused by:
sol: The layer of air around Earth has weight that presses down on everything.

q: Which of the following is not an effect of force?
sol: Mass is an intrinsic property and does not change when force is applied.

q: The atmosphere extends up to many ________ above the Earth's surface.
sol: The air envelope (atmosphere) reaches hundreds of kilometers high.

q: Shoulder bags are provided with broad straps to:
sol: Broad straps spread the weight over a larger area of the shoulder.

q: Forces acting on an object in the same direction ________ to each other.
sol: Net force is the sum of forces acting in the same direction.

q: Which of the following is a contact force?
sol: Friction occurs only when two surfaces touch.

q: Liquids exert pressure on the ________ of the container.
sol: Fluids exert pressure in all directions.

q: The force per unit area is called:
sol: This is the mathematical definition of pressure.

q: Gravity is:
sol: Gravity is always an attractive (pulling) force.

q: Friction depends on the ________ of the surfaces.
sol: Rougher surfaces produce more friction.

q: What happens when you go higher up in the mountains?
sol: The column of air above you becomes shorter and thinner, so pressure drops.

q: A goalkeeper stops a ball by applying:
sol: Using hands involves the use of muscles.

q: Two boys are pushing a box from opposite sides with 10N and 15N. What is the net force?
sol: 15N - 10N = 5N in the direction of the stronger force.

q: Magnetic force can be ________.
sol: Like poles repel, unlike poles attract.

q: Sucking a drink through a straw works because of:
sol: Sucking reduces air pressure in the straw; atmospheric pressure then pushes the liquid up.

q: A spring balance is used to measure:
sol: Spring balances measure the force applied (usually weight due to gravity).

q: The pressure of the atmosphere at sea level is approximately:
sol: Standard atmospheric pressure is about 101.3 kPa.

q: Force of friction can be reduced by using:
sol: Lubricants (oil, grease) create a thin layer between surfaces to reduce friction.

q: When we press a rubber sucker on a smooth surface, it sticks because of:
sol: Air is pushed out from under the sucker, and outside air pressure holds it tight.

q: The strength of a force is usually expressed by its:
sol: Magnitude refers to the size or amount of force applied.

q: The walls of a dam are made thicker at the bottom because:
o: Water pressure is higher at the bottom
Water pressure is higher at the top
sol: Dams must withstand the increased liquid pressure at greater depths.

q: Which force helps in writing on paper?
sol: Friction between the pen tip and paper allows ink to be deposited.

q: An interaction of one object with another object results in a ________ between the two objects.
sol: At least two objects must interact for a force to come into play.

q: If the area is doubled while keeping force constant, the pressure becomes:
sol: Since P = F/A, doubling A halves the pressure.

q: Force can change the state of ________ of an object.
sol: Force can make a stationary object move or a moving object stop.

q: A brick exerts more pressure when it is placed on its:
sol: Smaller area (thinnest side) results in higher pressure for the same weight.

q: Gases also exert ________ on the walls of their container.
sol: Gas molecules collide with container walls, creating pressure.

q: Iron nails are pointed to:
sol: The pointed tip has a small area, making it easy to drive into wood with high pressure.

q: The magnitude of atmospheric pressure is:
sol: Atmospheric pressure is enormous, but we don't feel it because our internal body pressure balances it.

q: Which force keeps the planets in orbit around the Sun?
sol: The Sun's gravity pulls on the planets.

q: A tug-of-war where neither team moves is an example of:
sol: Net force is zero when forces are balanced.

q: We are not crushed by atmospheric pressure because:
o: Pressure inside our body is equal to atmospheric pressure
sol: Fluids in our body exert an equal outward pressure.

q: Rolling friction is ________ than sliding friction.
sol: It is easier to roll an object than to slide it.

q: Force applied by a magnet on an iron nail is:
sol: Magnetism acts through space.

q: 1 Newton is approximately the weight of an object of mass:
sol: On Earth, 100g (0.1kg) has a weight of ~1N (Weight = m * g).

q: A loaded truck exerts ________ pressure on the road than an empty truck.
sol: More weight (force) means more pressure.

q: Which of the following is not a unit of force?
sol: Pascal is a unit of pressure, not force.

q: Two forces of 5N and 5N act on an object in the same direction. Net force is:
Deep sea divers wear special suits to protect themselves from:
sol: Water pressure at great depths is high enough to crush a human body.

q: The force exerted by a charged body on another charged or uncharged body is:
sol: This is the definition of electrostatic force.

q: A blunt knife does not cut fruit easily because:
o: It has a large area of contact
It has a small area of contact
sol: Blunt edge increases area, which decreases pressure.

q: When a ball is thrown up, which force slows it down?
sol: Gravity pulls the ball back toward Earth.

q: The use of ball bearings ________ friction.
sol: Ball bearings convert sliding friction into rolling friction.

q: If the net force on an object is zero, the object:
o: Will maintain its state of rest or uniform motion
sol: This is according to Newton's First Law (Law of Inertia).

q: A water tank is leaking from three holes at different heights. Pressure is highest at:
sol: Pressure increases with depth.

q: Friction is a ________ force.
sol: Friction requires physical interaction between surfaces.

q: The pressure at any point in a liquid at rest is ________ in all directions.
sol: Liquid pressure is transmitted equally in all directions (Pascal's Law).

q: Skiers use long flat skis to:
sol: Long skis have a large area, preventing the skier from sinking into the snow.

q: Which force helps in holding a glass of water?
sol: Friction between the hand and glass prevents it from slipping.

q: The weight of an object is actually the ________ acting on it.
sol: Weight = Mass × Acceleration due to gravity.

q: Pressure is ________ proportional to force.
sol: More force results in more pressure for the same area.

q: Opening a door by pulling the handle is an example of applying:
sol: Pulling is a type of force application.

q: A bicycle comes to a stop when we stop pedaling because of:
sol: Friction between tires and road, and in the axles, slows it down.

q: If you blow air into a balloon, it expands because:
sol: Air molecules hit the inside walls, pushing them outward.

q: Which of the following is not a non-contact force?
sol: Friction is a contact force.

q: A man standing on two feet exerts pressure P. If he stands on one foot, pressure is:
sol: Area is halved, so pressure is doubled.

q: The state of motion is described by its:
sol: Velocity includes both magnitude (speed) and direction.

q: When we stop rowing a boat, it stops after some distance due to:
sol: Water resistance (drag) is a form of friction.

q: An apple falls from a tree because of:
sol: The Earth exerts a gravitational pull on the apple.

q: Force can change the ________ of a moving car when brakes are applied.
sol: Braking force reduces the speed.

q: Which of the following does not exert pressure?
sol: A vacuum contains no matter to exert force or pressure.

q: The force that keeps us on the ground is:
sol: Gravity pulls everything toward the center of the Earth.

q: A force is applied on a box, but it does not move. The net force is:
sol: If the object remains stationary, the applied force is balanced by friction.

q: To reduce pressure on the ground, a building should have:
sol: Wide foundation increases area, reducing pressure on the soil.

q: Atmospheric pressure ________ as we go to higher altitudes.
sol: Air density and the height of the air column above both decrease.

q: Which of the following is not a characteristic of force?
sol: Force cannot alter the amount of matter (mass) in an object.

q: The pressure exerted by air on us is called:
sol: This is the specific term for air pressure from the atmosphere.

q: Fluid is a common name for:
sol: Fluids are substances that can flow (liquids and gases).

q: Pressure at a given depth in a liquid ________ on the density of the liquid.
sol: P = hρg (where ρ is density).

q: An example of non-contact force is:
sol: Electrostatic forces act through an electric field.

q: Force changes the motion of an object only when it is:
sol: Balanced forces result in no change in motion.

q: Which force pulls a fallen leaf to the ground?
sol: Earth's gravity pulls the leaf down.

q: Pressure is measured in N/m². This unit is also known as:
sol: Named after Blaise Pascal.

q: Walking on a slippery floor is difficult because of:
sol: Low friction makes it hard to get a grip.

q: If you increase force while keeping area constant, pressure will:
sol: Pressure is directly proportional to force.

q: A car is moving with a constant speed in a straight line. The net force is:
sol: Constant velocity means zero acceleration, hence zero net force.

q: Which of the following is responsible for wear and tear of machine parts?
sol: Friction between moving parts causes heat and physical damage.

q: The pressure inside our body is ________ the atmospheric pressure.
sol: This equilibrium prevents us from being crushed.

q: A stone is falling through water. Which forces are acting on it?
sol: Gravity pulls it down while water resistance opposes the motion.
